preamble: The phonetic transformation of a voiced palatal plosive /ɟ/ into a voiced palatal approximant /j/ is a well-documented historical sound change, primarily understood as a form of lenition. This process involves a 'weakening' of the consonant, where the complete obstruction of airflow characteristic of a plosive is reduced to a partial obstruction, resulting in an approximant. This shift often occurs in specific phonetic environments, such as intervocalically, where consonants tend to be less strongly articulated.

examples.note: In the Csángó dialect of Hungarian, a phonetic shift involving the spirantization (weakening to an approximant) of /ɟ/ to /j/ has been observed.
